reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher autonomy, workload realism, and professional judgment in assessment design. Documented union positions on assessment redesign as performance-audit creep (round 06 episodic memory). Professional interest in assessment validity and resistance to high-stakes sorting at age 10.
reactions.interest: Pedagogus unions and classroom teachers — their workload, assessment autonomy, and professional standing in reformed selection channels.
reactions.public_good_frame: A selection mechanism that respects teacher judgment, does not expand informal high-stakes assessment burden, and does not treat assessment redesign as a surveillance or performance-audit tool.
reactions.argument: S1 conditions my stance on three grounds: (1) The scenario explicitly acknowledges that pedagogus unions treat any assessment redesign as performance-audit creep absent explicit decoupling (documented in round 06). Redesigning the central admission exam is redesign, and the scenario does not propose decoupling from performance audit or retention of teacher-led classroom assessment as the primary evaluation mode. (2) The psychometric validation timeline (year 1–3) creates a three-year workload burden on pilot schools and assessment designers; this falls on teachers and is not quantified or budgeted. (3) The scenario itself warns that 'reformed or scarcer selection channels may invite informal selection (interviews, reputation) that reproduces SES sorting' — this shifts the sorting burden from a transparent exam onto school-based judgment, which expands the informal assessment load on teachers and exposes them to bias and liability claims without additional training or autonomy protections. Conditional support is contingent on: (i) explicit legislative decoupling of the new assessment from performance audit of schools or teachers; (ii) a binding commitment that classroom-based assessment by teachers remains the primary evaluation mode and is not subordinated to the central exam; (iii) full budgeting and union-negotiated compensation for pilot-school and assessment-design workload; (iv) safeguards against informal selection (interviews, reputation) replacing the transparent exam, or if such informal channels cannot be prevented, statutory limits on their use and teacher liability protection.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support would increase if: (a) the reform explicitly decouples the redesigned assessment from any school or teacher performance-audit use; (b) union and teacher input is contractually required at every stage of psychometric design; (c) pilot schools receive full workload reduction and compensation; (d) informal selection channels are legally prohibited or, if unavoidable, ringfenced with teacher liability protection. Opposition would solidify if: assessment redesign proceeds as a de facto performance-audit lever, or if informal selection (interviews, reputation) replaces the transparent exam without teacher safeguards.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher workload and employment stability in a decade-long phase-down that maintains parallel regimes (grandfathered and capped cohorts). Professional interest in predictable student composition and teaching conditions across the transition. Concern for general-school teachers absorbing retained high-achievers without additional training or support.
reactions.interest: Classroom teachers in general schools and state-maintained selective schools — their stability, manageable class composition during transition, and training for changed peer groups.
reactions.public_good_frame: A phased reduction that does not destabilise teacher employment, does not create a decade-long two-tier teaching environment, and provides general-school teachers with support to teach increasingly mixed cohorts.
reactions.argument: S2 triggers conditional resistance on workload and feasibility grounds: (1) The scenario estimates 'medium and sustained' administrative cost across a decade, not a transitional burden. This sustained parallel regime — grandfathered selective cohorts running alongside capped general-school intake — splits the teaching workforce into two regimes (teachers managing traditional selective tracks vs. teachers managing expanded, more mixed general-school cohorts). Workload is sustained, not transitional, because teachers in general schools must simultaneously teach rising proportions of high-achievers (the 'peer-composition benefit') without training or class-size reduction. (2) The scenario states that 'capacity expansion in general-school upper grades to absorb retained high-achievers' is required but does not budget or commit to class-size management; teachers absorb compositional change with no explicit relief. (3) The scenario explicitly acknowledges that 'non-state maintainers expand selective intake to absorb displaced demand, defeating the cap' — this means the cap does not reduce early sorting overall, it merely shifts it to church/foundation schools, leaving state-school teachers managing a decade-long misalignment between official caps and actual market sorting. Conditional support is contingent on: (i) a binding agreement with non-state maintainers (church/foundation) to prevent offsetting expansion, with enforcement mechanisms; (ii) class-size reduction in general-school upper grades receiving retained high-achievers, funded and enforced; (iii) mandatory teacher retraining in mixed-ability instruction, funded and staffed; (iv) transitional protection for selective-school teachers (no involuntary reassignment, job guarantees until grandfathered cohorts exit).
reactions.condition_to_change: Support would increase if: (a) non-state maintainers bind themselves to parallel caps with enforcement (not merely voluntary negotiation); (b) class sizes in general-school upper grades are statutorily capped at lower levels; (c) teacher retraining is fully funded and does not rely on unpaid professional development. Opposition would solidify if: the cap is not binding on non-state maintainers, or if parallel-regime costs are absorbed as unpaid workload by general-school teachers.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher supply constraint (documented strong in implementation_planning and OECD analysis). Professional autonomy and workload concerns around differentiated instruction without adequate training or support. Documented union resistance to large-scale structural reform that displaces existing job structures.
reactions.interest: Classroom teachers and pedagogus unions — their employment security, training adequacy, and classroom workload under a comprehensive-school model that requires differentiated instruction at scale.
reactions.public_good_frame: Large-scale structural reform is only sustainable if the teacher workforce is stable, adequately trained, and not degraded in working conditions. Hungary currently lacks the teacher supply and diagnostic-support infrastructure that made the Finnish transition viable.
reactions.argument: S3 merits opposition on grounds of infeasibility and workload burden: (1) The scenario acknowledges that 'teacher shortage makes redistribution across comprehensive schools infeasible in the short term, degrading academic quality' (tagged strong in implementation_planning). This is not a surmountable constraint — it is a binding feasibility limit. Hungary currently has acute teacher shortages in both urban and rural areas; comprehensive schooling to age 14 requires nationwide retraining in differentiated instruction, which cannot occur while the workforce is already depleted. The scenario proposes 'nationwide teacher retraining in differentiated instruction — high; no per-cohort/per-district unit cost available' — this is a placeholder, not a plan. Without specifying how many teachers, at what cost, and how instruction continues during retraining, this is not implementable. (2) Differentiated instruction within a comprehensive school requires diagnostic and remedial support systems ('Finnish-style') that Hungary does not have. The scenario lists this as 'high, recurring' cost but does not commit funding or personnel. Teachers in comprehensive schools will be expected to deliver differentiation without the diagnostic infrastructure, coaching, or class-size reduction that makes it feasible. This expands workload without commensurate support. (3) The scenario assumes 'comprehensive schools can be equipped with the teacher quality and diagnostic support that made the Finnish transition work' — but this is explicitly contradicted by the scenario's own statement that 'Hungary currently lacks this'. This is an assumption failure: the reform is predicated on a condition that is not met and is not budgeted to be met. (4) The political-risk section notes 'reversal after an electoral cycle, as in Poland 2016–2019, despite measured gains'. Poland's reversal was triggered in part by public concern about academic quality during transition — this risk is real and documented. If the reform begins with teacher shortage degrading academic quality, reversal becomes likely, leaving teachers in a destabilised and demoralised state. (5) Pedagogus unions have shown sustained opposition to large-scale structural reform that displaces job categories and redistributes the workforce. S3 requires wholesale redistribution of selective-school teachers into comprehensive schools, with no protection for those whose specialisms (selective-track teaching) cease to exist.
reactions.condition_to_change: Opposition would shift to conditional support if and only if: (a) Hungary first stabilises its teacher workforce (recruitment, retention, salary parity with comparators) to above current shortfall levels, with a binding multi-year commitment; (b) a fully costed, time-sequenced plan for retraining in differentiated instruction is legislated, with full salary coverage during retraining and no cuts to instructional hours; (c) diagnostic and remedial support systems are built and staffed before the first comprehensive cohort enters (not during). (d) a binding agreement with non-state maintainers prevents sorting migration to private schools, with enforcement; (e) selective-school teachers are guaranteed retraining, voluntary transition, or job security until retirement; (f) class-size limits are statutorily set at 20 or lower in comprehensive upper grades. Without all of these, the reform is unworkable and will fail on teacher supply and workload grounds.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Professional interest in stable working conditions and sustainable curriculum standards. Concern that targeted funding without structural change does not address root causes of sorting, and that teachers in disadvantaged schools will be held accountable for outcomes they cannot control. Documented union interest in curriculum autonomy and resistance to standards-based audit.
reactions.interest: Classroom teachers in disadvantaged general schools, and union interest in curriculum autonomy — their working conditions, accountability frameworks, and whether standards become de facto performance-audit tools.
reactions.public_good_frame: Compensation for early sorting is legitimate only if it does not increase teacher accountability for outcomes driven by structural inequality, and only if teachers retain professional autonomy in curriculum delivery and assessment.
reactions.argument: S4 merits conditional support because it avoids structural disruption but carries serious risks for teacher autonomy and workload: (1) The scenario proposes 'rigorous national curriculum standards as guidance, not audit triggers' — but this distinction is fragile in practice. In other countries (Portugal, Spain), curriculum standards become de facto audit triggers through inspection, parent complaint, and league-table publication. Teachers in disadvantaged schools, facing higher concentrations of low-SES pupils (because selection remains intact), will be held accountable for attainment gaps they cannot close via standards compliance alone. The scenario acknowledges this: 'compensation can offset, not just mask, the effects of early sorting' [assumption — unverifiable ex ante]. If compensation does not offset sorting, teachers become the accountability sink. (2) The scenario notes 'budget vulnerability: targeted funding is the first line cut in fiscal consolidation, undermining durability' — this is a critical workload risk. If TEIP-style funding is cut mid-cycle, teachers in disadvantaged schools are left with expanded expectations (higher standards, more mixed cohorts due to sorting) but no resources. Workload expands, accountability tightens, funding evaporates. (3) The scenario states that 'early partition persists, the root cause — selection at age 10–12 concentrating low-SES pupils in general schools — is not addressed, only mitigated'. Teachers in those general schools inherit the compositional effect of sorting without the structural change that would reduce it. They teach classes with lower peer composition and higher remedial need, while being held to the same curriculum standards as selective-school peers. This is a classic accountability trap. (4) Teacher shortage is tagged 'strong' as a constraint: 'Teacher shortage undermines curriculum-standard and support delivery in disadvantaged schools.' If teachers are scarce, they cluster in better-resourced schools; disadvantaged schools get less experienced, less qualified teachers. Standards-based accountability applied to less-qualified teachers in under-resourced schools is unfair. Conditional support is contingent on: (i) explicit legislative language decoupling curriculum standards from school/teacher performance audit (standards are guidance, not triggers for inspection or sanction); (ii) binding, multi-year funding commitments that cannot be cut in fiscal consolidation, with enforcement (e.g. constitutional reserve); (iii) union co-design of accountability frameworks to ensure they do not attribute sorting-driven gaps to teacher quality; (iv) class-size caps and specialist staffing (remedial teachers, counsellors) in disadvantaged schools, funded and enforced; (v) a binding evaluation clause: if high-achiever retention in funded general schools does not improve outcomes for non-selected pupils within five years, the policy is reviewed for structural change.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support would increase if: (a) standards are statutorily decoupled from performance audit, with inspection explicitly prohibited from using standards as accountability triggers; (b) targeted funding is constitutionally protected and cannot be cut; (c) teacher unions approve the accountability framework as fair to teachers in disadvantaged schools. Opposition would solidify if: standards become audit triggers, funding is cut, or the evaluation shows that compensation does not offset sorting — in that case, the case for structural change becomes stronger.
